title: Extract a Substring Using the Reference
prompt_md:
## Goal
A language reference is the catalog you consult to use an unfamiliar function. Here is the reference entry for `std::string::substr`:

```
basic_string substr( size_type pos = 0, size_type count = npos ) const;
```

**Returns:** a new string of up to `count` characters starting at position `pos`. If `count` runs past the end of the string, only the available characters are returned.

Use this function to extract part of an input word.

## Input
- Line 1: a single word (1-100 characters, no spaces)
- Line 2: two integers `start` and `length`

## Output
Two lines:
- the extracted substring
- the number of characters in that substring

`start` is always a valid index; `length` may extend past the end of the word.

## Constraints
- Produce the substring with `substr`, not a manual character loop.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- length extends past end of string

title: Look Up and Use std::string::find
prompt_md:
## Goal
Here is the reference entry for a function you may not have used:

```
size_type find( const basic_string& str, size_type pos = 0 ) const;
```

**Returns:** the index of the first occurrence of `str` in this string (searching from `pos`), or `std::string::npos` if `str` does not occur. `std::string::npos` is a sentinel constant meaning "not found".

Use this function to locate one word inside another.

## Input
- Line 1: the haystack string (single word, 1-200 characters)
- Line 2: the needle string (single word, 1-50 characters)

## Output
One line:
- `Found at position <index>` if the needle occurs in the haystack (0-based index)
- `Not found` otherwise

## Constraints
- Use `find` and compare its result against `std::string::npos`; do not search character by character.
